steps.title: Spot the appositive that needs punctuation
steps.content:
The sentence has two ways to describe the person: “essayist” (her role) and “author of Of Landscape and Longing“ (her published work).

The phrase “author of Of Landscape and Longing“ is an appositive—it provides additional information about the noun it follows.

Appositives must be set off by commas when they provide non-essential detail.

steps.title: Match punctuation to structure
steps.content:
”Essayist Carolyn Servid“ forms a complete noun phrase: the role followed by the name that identifies which essayist. Because the proper noun restrictively identifies which essayist (no comma needed between them), the noun phrase stays tight. The comma then belongs after “Servid,” immediately before the appositive “author of…” to properly set it off.

Options A and B fail to place a comma before the appositive, leaving “author of” unpunctuated. Option D unnecessarily separates “Carolyn Servid” with commas, treating it as a standalone appositive when it already restrictively identifies the essayist.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
